1: Who has believed what we proclaimed? — the opening question of the fourth Servant Song. Aman (believe/trust, disp 2): to believe the report is to entrust oneself to the message's truthfulness and to the one it describes. The question implies that the report is not self-evidently persuasive — the Servant's manner does not commend itself to natural observation (v 2). The arm of the LORD links back to Isaiah 51:5, 9; Isaiah 52:10: the same redemptive power the exiles prayed for has been revealed — but in a form that was not expected. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:16 to explain Israel's unbelief at the gospel proclamation.
2: The Servant's origins: like a tender shoot from dry soil, no attractive form or majesty to draw attention. The dry soil and absence of beauty are deliberate anti-messianic markers — this figure does not fit the kingly warrior expectation. The commentary of John 1:46 (Can anything good come out of Nazareth?) and 1 Corinthians 1:23 (a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles) inhabit exactly this gap between expectation and reality.
3: The Servant is despised and rejected by people — a man who yada suffering (knew/was familiar with, disp 2). Yada here is experiential knowledge — intimate acquaintance with suffering, not mere observation. Like one from whom people hide their faces: the Servant's suffering is so severe that observers avert their eyes. We held him of no account — the communal speakers confess that they dismissed the Servant. This verse is the turning point where the speakers recognize their error.
4: Yet he was the one who took up our sicknesses and carried our pain. The contrast (aken — surely, yet) corrects the misreading: we thought he was being struck by God; in fact he was bearing ours. The taking-up vocabulary (nasa) is the same used for the scapegoat (Leviticus 16:22) and for priestly sin-bearing. Matthew quotes this verse (Matthew 8:17) applying it to Jesus' healing ministry — the bearing of infirmities was the pattern of the Servant's entire ministry, not only his death.
5: He was pierced because of our transgressions, crushed because of our iniquities. The preposition is min + cause — his wounds are directly caused by our sin. The punishment that brought us shalom was upon him (shalom, disp 2): the chastisement (musar) that produces comprehensive wellbeing for us fell on him. By his wounds we are healed — the healing is grounded in the wounding. Shalom here is not merely peace as reconciliation but the full restoration of every dimension of life that was broken by sin. This is the most direct substitutionary statement in the Hebrew Bible; the NT applies it to the atonement at 1 Peter 2:24.
6: The communal confession: we all went astray like sheep; each turned to his own way. The sheep-going-astray image is universal and specific — the aimless wandering of the sheep who has no shepherd. The LORD laid on him the iniquity of us all — the active subject is YHWH, and the verb (hifgiya) means to cause to meet, to lay upon. The cosmic transaction is not the Servant's unilateral act but YHWH's purposeful placing of the community's collective guilt upon the Servant. This is the heart of substitutionary atonement.
7: The Servant's silence under oppression: he never opened his mouth. The doubled simile — lamb to slaughter, sheep before shearers — emphasizes absolute non-resistance. This is not weakness but purposeful submission to the role YHWH has assigned. Philip uses this verse in Acts 8:32-35 to explain the gospel to the Ethiopian eunuch; the early church consistently read the silence as Jesus' silence before Pilate (Mark 14:61, Isaiah 15:5).
8: By a perversion of justice he was taken away — a legal inversion; the just one is condemned by unjust process. Who gave any thought to his fate? — his generation did not consider the meaning of what was happening. He was cut off from the land of the living — the language of violent premature death. Because of my people's transgression, the stroke fell on him — again the substitutionary logic: the stroke aimed at the transgressors meets the Servant instead.
9: He was assigned a grave among the wicked, yet his burial was with the rich. The double burial notice corresponds to the paradox of the entire song: the Servant ends where he does not deserve to end because his death was not for his own sins. He had done no violence, and no deceit was in his mouth — the Servant's moral innocence is explicit. The NT applies v 9 to Jesus' burial in Joseph of Arimathea's tomb (Matthew 27:57-60).
10: Yet it was the LORD's will to crush him and make him suffer. The crushing is YHWH's purposive act, not merely permitted tragedy. When his nefesh (soul/life, disp 3) is given as a guilt offering (asham) — the asham is the reparation offering of Lev 5-6, given when one has incurred liability by trespassing on what belongs to another. The Servant's nefesh — his whole self, his life — is the reparation that settles the debt. The result: he will see offspring, prolong his days, and YHWH's purpose will prosper. Life follows the asham-death; vindication follows crushing.
11: Out of the anguish of his nefesh (soul, disp 3) he will see and be satisfied. The sight after suffering — the Servant sees the result of his asham and is content. By his knowledge my righteous servant will make many righteous — yada knowledge (disp 2): the Servant's experiential knowing, the same knowing-suffering of v 3, is now the instrument of justification. He bears their iniquities (yisbol — same root as v 4). The many who are made righteous are the counterpart of the we all who went astray (v 6).
12: Therefore I will give him a portion among the great, and he will share the plunder with the strong — because he poured out his nefesh (soul, disp 3) to death and was counted among the transgressors. Three grounds for the vindication-reward: he poured out his nefesh, he was numbered with rebels, he bore the sin of many and made intercession for transgressors. The intercession continues after the atoning death — the Servant's advocacy does not end at the cross. Luke 22:37 quotes this verse as Jesus' own self-interpretation at the Last Supper.
